classification.domain: natural products for resistant H. pylori

topic.what: In-vitro study demonstrating that Chios mastic gum retains antibacterial activity against drug-resistant H. pylori strains, including clarithromycin-resistant isolates that evade standard eradication therapy.
topic.how: MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration) assays performed against a panel of H. pylori clinical isolates with characterized antibiotic resistance profiles; mastic gum extracts compared against standard antibiotics.
topic.why_matters: Clarithromycin resistance is the primary driver of H. pylori eradication failure globally; a natural product that bypasses this resistance mechanism opens adjunct or alternative treatment possibilities, paralleling TCM findings (Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang) and reinforcing cross-cultural convergence on natural antibacterials.

characteristics.what_it_measures: MIC of mastic gum against clarithromycin-resistant clinical isolates
characteristics.value: Inhibitory activity maintained against resistant strains at comparable MIC to sensitive strains
characteristics.vs_baseline: Clarithromycin MIC in resistant isolates (>1 µg/mL defining resistance)
characteristics.evidence: MIC panel across multiple resistant and sensitive isolates
characteristics.context: In vitro only; clinical pharmacokinetics and gastric delivery not addressed

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number and type of drug-resistant strains inhibited
characteristics.value: Active against multiple resistance profiles including clarithromycin and metronidazole resistant strains
characteristics.evidence: Panels of clinically isolated strains with confirmed resistance genotypes/phenotypes
characteristics.context: Resistance profiles confirmed by standard susceptibility testing

characteristics.what_it_measures: Alignment of mastic efficacy with TCM Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang anti-H. pylori findings
characteristics.value: Both traditional systems produce anti-H. pylori activity via distinct phytochemical scaffolds
characteristics.vs_baseline: Independent ethnomedical traditions converging on similar outcome
characteristics.evidence: Parallel literature: Tohda 2002 (Ban Xia Xie Xin Tang) vs this study
characteristics.context: Cross-cultural comparison is editorial inference; not directly tested in this paper

good_for: Patients failing standard clarithromycin-based triple therapy
Regions with high clarithromycin resistance prevalence
Natural product drug development targeting resistant H. pylori
Combinatorial strategies pairing mastic with existing antibiotics for synergy
not_for: Primary eradication therapy without clinical trial support
Patients with resin or terpene hypersensitivity
requires: In-vivo and clinical validation against resistant strains
Pharmacokinetic studies on mastic acid delivery to gastric mucosa
Synergy testing with current rescue regimens (bismuth quadruple, levofloxacin-based)

novelty: Demonstrates mastic gum efficacy specifically against antibiotic-resistant H. pylori, filling a gap left by the original Paraschos 2007 animal study
Positions mastic as a resistance-agnostic natural antibacterial with novel mechanism not shared by macrolides
Suggests Greco-Roman and East Asian traditional GI remedies may share resistance-bypassing potential through different phytochemical classes
open_problems: Mechanism by which triterpenic acids overcome clarithromycin resistance (efflux pump inhibition? membrane disruption?) unknown
No clinical data in antibiotic-resistant H. pylori patients
Bioavailability of active mastic components in the stomach under realistic clinical conditions not established
Optimal mastic formulation (raw gum, extract, capsule) for resistant strain eradication not determined
